revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shintōshū]][[fr:Shintōshū]][[id:Shintōshū]][[ja:神道集]][[pt:Shintōshū]][[ru:Синтосю]][[uk:Сінтосю]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q2616931]] Wikidata name: Shintōshū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shintōshū'''''|神道集}} is a Japanese ''[[setsuwa]]'' collection in ten volumes,{{sfnm|1a1=Murakami|1y=1983|1p=499}} believed to date from the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] (1336–1392).<ref name=kojien>Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> It illustrates with tales about various shrines the [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory, according to which Japanese ''[[kami]]'' were simply local manifestations of the Indian gods of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. This theory, created and developed mostly by [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] monks, was never systematized but was nonetheless very pervasive and influential.<ref>* {{cite web |url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm |title=Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami |last=Scheid, Bernhard |author-link=Bernhard Scheid |language=de |access-date=June 13, 2011}}</ref> The book had thereafter great influence over literature and the arts.<ref name="kojien"/>
==History== The book is believed to have been written during the late Nanboku-chō period, either during the [[:en:Bunna|Bunna]] or the [[:en:Enbun|Enbun]] era.<ref name=yahoo>{{cite web|last=Murakami|first=Manabu|title=Shintōshū|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E9%81%93%E9%9B%86/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121209055811/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E9%81%93%E9%9B%86/|url-status=dead|archive-date=9 December 2012|work=Shogakukan Encyclopedia|publisher=Yahoo Japan|access-date=15 June 2011|language=ja}}</ref> It carries the note {{nihongo|Agui-saku|安居院作|extra=made by Agui}} but who exactly wrote it is unclear. Divided into ten volumes and 50 chapters, it supports the Tendai and [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shinto]] ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory according to which Japanese ''[[kami]]'' were simply local manifestations of the Indian gods of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. This theory was never systematized but became nonetheless the most important tool through which foreign Buddhism was reconciled with local ''kami'' beliefs. The book illustrates it through tales dedicated to various shrines and to the Buddhist gods which are the true nature of the ''kami'' they enshrine.<ref name="yahoo"/> It deals mostly with shrines located west of [[Tonegawa, Kōzuke|Tonegawa]] in [[:en:Kōzuke province|Kōzuke province]] (like Akagi Daimyōjin, Ikaho Daimyōjin and Komochiyama Daimyōjin), the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] and other [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] shrines, explaining the reason for their ''kami's'' rebirths, and telling tales about their previous lives.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The common point of the tales is that before being reborn as a tutelary ''kami'' of an area, a person must first be born and suffer there as a human being. The suffering is mostly caused by relationships with relatives, especially wives or husbands.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The book had a great impact on the literature and arts of the following centuries.
==References== {{Reflist}}
=== Works cited === {{Refbegin|colwidth=80em}} * {{cite book |last = Murakami |first = Manabu |author-link = Manabu Murakami (scholar) |chapter = ''Shintōshū'' |pages = 499 |title = [[Nihon Koten Bungaku Daijiten]] |script-title = ja:日本古典文学大辞典 |language = ja |year = 1983 |volume = 3 |location = Tokyo |publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten]] |oclc = 11917421 }} {{Refend}}
{{Jmyth navbox long}}

}}

This theory, created and developed mostly by [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] monks, was never systematized but was nonetheless very pervasive and influential.<ref>* {{cite web |url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm |title=Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami |last=Scheid, Bernhard |author-link=Bernhard Scheid |language=de |access-date=June 13, 2011}}</ref> The book had thereafter great influence over literature and the arts.<ref name="kojien"/>

Divided into ten volumes and 50 chapters, it supports the Tendai and [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shinto]] ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory according to which Japanese ''[[kami]]'' were simply local manifestations of the Indian gods of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. The common point of the tales is that before being reborn as a tutelary ''kami'' of an area, a person must first be born and suffer there as a human being. The suffering is mostly caused by relationships with relatives, especially wives or husbands.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The book had a great impact on the literature and arts of the following centuries.
==References== {{Reflist}}
=== Works cited === {{Refbegin|colwidth=80em}} * {{cite book |last = Murakami |first = Manabu |author-link = Manabu Murakami (scholar) |chapter = ''Shintōshū'' |pages = 499 |title = [[Nihon Koten Bungaku Daijiten]] |script-title = ja:日本古典文学大辞典 |language = ja |year = 1983 |volume = 3 |location = Tokyo |publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten]] |oclc = 11917421 }} {{Refend}}

The book illustrates it through tales dedicated to various shrines and to the Buddhist gods which are the true nature of the ''kami'' they enshrine.<ref name="yahoo"/> It deals mostly with shrines located west of [[Tonegawa, Kōzuke|Tonegawa]] in [[:en:Kōzuke province|Kōzuke province]] (like Akagi Daimyōjin, Ikaho Daimyōjin and Komochiyama Daimyōjin), the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] and other [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] shrines, explaining the reason for their ''kami's'' rebirths, and telling tales about their previous lives.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The common point of the tales is that before being reborn as a tutelary ''kami'' of an area, a person must first be born and suffer there as a human being. The suffering is mostly caused by relationships with relatives, especially wives or husbands.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The book had a great impact on the literature and arts of the following centuries.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shintōshū]][[fr:Shintōshū]][[id:Shintōshū]][[ja:神道集]][[pt:Shintōshū]][[ru:Синтосю]][[uk:Сінтосю]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2616931]] Wikidata name: Shintōshū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shintōshū'''''|神道集}} is a Japanese ''[[setsuwa]]'' collection in ten volumes,{{sfnm|1a1=Murakami|1y=1983|1p=499}} believed to date from the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] (1336–1392).<ref name=kojien>Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> It illustrates with tales about various shrines the [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory, according to which Japanese ''[[kami]]'' were simply local manifestations of the Indian gods of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. This theory, created and developed mostly by [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] monks, was never systematized but was nonetheless very pervasive and influential.<ref>* {{cite web |url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm |title=Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami |last=Scheid, Bernhard |author-link=Bernhard Scheid |language=de |access-date=June 13, 2011}}</ref> The book had thereafter great influence over literature and the arts.<ref name="kojien"/>
==History== The book is believed to have been written during the late Nanboku-chō period, either during the [[:en:Bunna|Bunna]] or the [[:en:Enbun|Enbun]] era.<ref name=yahoo>{{cite web|last=Murakami|first=Manabu|title=Shintōshū|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E9%81%93%E9%9B%86/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121209055811/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E9%81%93%E9%9B%86/|url-status=dead|archive-date=9 December 2012|work=Shogakukan Encyclopedia|publisher=Yahoo Japan|access-date=15 June 2011|language=ja}}</ref> It carries the note {{nihongo|Agui-saku|安居院作|extra=made by Agui}} but who exactly wrote it is unclear. Divided into ten volumes and 50 chapters, it supports the Tendai and [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shinto]] ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory according to which Japanese ''[[kami]]'' were simply local manifestations of the Indian gods of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. This theory was never systematized but became nonetheless the most important tool through which foreign Buddhism was reconciled with local ''kami'' beliefs. The common point of the tales is that before being reborn as a tutelary ''kami'' of an area, a person must first be born and suffer there as a human being. The suffering is mostly caused by relationships with relatives, especially wives or husbands.<ref name="yahoo"/>

=== Works cited === {{Refbegin|colwidth=80em}} * {{cite book |last = Murakami |first = Manabu |author-link = Manabu Murakami (scholar) |chapter = ''Shintōshū'' |pages = 499 |title = [[Nihon Koten Bungaku Daijiten]] |script-title = ja:日本古典文学大辞典 |language = ja |year = 1983 |volume = 3 |location = Tokyo |publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten]] |oclc = 11917421 }} {{Refend}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shintōshū]][[fr:Shintōshū]][[id:Shintōshū]][[ja:神道集]][[pt:Shintōshū]][[ru:Синтосю]][[uk:Сінтосю]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2616931]] Wikidata name: Shintōshū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shintōshū'''''|神道集}} is a Japanese ''[[setsuwa]]'' collection in ten volumes,{{sfnm|1a1=Murakami|1y=1983|1p=499}} believed to date from the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] (1336–1392).<ref name=kojien>Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> It illustrates with tales about various shrines the [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory, according to which Japanese ''[[kami]]'' were simply local manifestations of the Indian gods of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. This theory, created and developed mostly by [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] monks, was never systematized but was nonetheless very pervasive and influential.<ref>* {{cite web |url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm |title=Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami |last=Scheid, Bernhard |author-link=Bernhard Scheid |language=de |access-date=June 13, 2011}}</ref> The book had thereafter great influence over literature and the arts.<ref name="kojien"/>
==History== The book is believed to have been written during the late Nanboku-chō period, either during the [[:en:Bunna|Bunna]] or the [[:en:Enbun|Enbun]] era.<ref name=yahoo>{{cite web|last=Murakami|first=Manabu|title=Shintōshū|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E9%81%93%E9%9B%86/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121209055811/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E9%81%93%E9%9B%86/|url-status=dead|archive-date=9 December 2012|work=Shogakukan Encyclopedia|publisher=Yahoo Japan|access-date=15 June 2011|language=ja}}</ref> It carries the note {{nihongo|Agui-saku|安居院作|extra=made by Agui}} but who exactly wrote it is unclear. Divided into ten volumes and 50 chapters, it supports the Tendai and [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shinto]] ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory according to which Japanese ''[[kami]]'' were simply local manifestations of the Indian gods of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. This theory was never systematized but became nonetheless the most important tool through which foreign Buddhism was reconciled with local ''kami'' beliefs. The book illustrates it through tales dedicated to various shrines and to the Buddhist gods which are the true nature of the ''kami'' they enshrine.<ref name="yahoo"/> It deals mostly with shrines located west of [[Tonegawa, Kōzuke|Tonegawa]] in [[:en:Kōzuke province|Kōzuke province]] (like Akagi Daimyōjin, Ikaho Daimyōjin and Komochiyama Daimyōjin), the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] and other [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] shrines, explaining the reason for their ''kami's'' rebirths, and telling tales about their previous lives.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The common point of the tales is that before being reborn as a tutelary ''kami'' of an area, a person must first be born and suffer there as a human being. The suffering is mostly caused by relationships with relatives, especially wives or husbands.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The book had a great impact on the literature and arts of the following centuries.
==References== {{Reflist}}
=== Works cited === {{Refbegin|colwidth=80em}} * {{cite book |last = Murakami |first = Manabu |author-link = Manabu Murakami (scholar) |chapter = ''Shintōshū'' |pages = 499 |title = [[Nihon Koten Bungaku Daijiten]] |script-title = ja:日本古典文学大辞典 |language = ja |year = 1983 |volume = 3 |location = Tokyo |publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten]] |oclc = 11917421 }} {{Refend}}

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Shintōshū]] [[id:Shintōshū]] [[ja:神道集]] [[pt:Shintōshū]] [[ru:Синтосю]] [[uk:Сінтосю]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q2616931}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shintōshū]][[fr:Shintōshū]][[id:Shintōshū]][[ja:神道集]][[pt:Shintōshū]][[ru:Синтосю]][[uk:Сінтосю]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shintōshū]][[fr:Shintōshū]][[id:Shintōshū]][[ja:神道集]][[pt:Shintōshū]][[ru:Синтосю]][[uk:Сінтосю]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2616931]] Wikidata name: Shintōshū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shintōshū'''''|神道集}} is a Japanese ''[[setsuwa]]'' collection in ten volumes,{{sfnm|1a1=Murakami|1y=1983|1p=499}} believed to date from the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] (1336–1392).<ref name=kojien>Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> It illustrates with tales about various shrines the [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory, according to which Japanese ''[[kami]]'' were simply local manifestations of the Indian gods of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. This theory, created and developed mostly by [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] monks, was never systematized but was nonetheless very pervasive and influential.<ref>* {{cite web |url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm |title=Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami |last=Scheid, Bernhard |author-link=Bernhard Scheid |language=de |access-date=June 13, 2011}}</ref> The book had thereafter great influence over literature and the arts.<ref name="kojien"/>
==History== The book is believed to have been written during the late Nanboku-chō period, either during the [[:en:Bunna|Bunna]] or the [[:en:Enbun|Enbun]] era.<ref name=yahoo>{{cite web|last=Murakami|first=Manabu|title=Shintōshū|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E9%81%93%E9%9B%86/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121209055811/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E9%81%93%E9%9B%86/|url-status=dead|archive-date=9 December 2012|work=Shogakukan Encyclopedia|publisher=Yahoo Japan|access-date=15 June 2011|language=ja}}</ref> It carries the note {{nihongo|Agui-saku|安居院作|extra=made by Agui}} but who exactly wrote it is unclear. Divided into ten volumes and 50 chapters, it supports the Tendai and [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shinto]] ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory according to which Japanese ''[[kami]]'' were simply local manifestations of the Indian gods of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. This theory was never systematized but became nonetheless the most important tool through which foreign Buddhism was reconciled with local ''kami'' beliefs. The book illustrates it through tales dedicated to various shrines and to the Buddhist gods which are the true nature of the ''kami'' they enshrine.<ref name="yahoo"/> It deals mostly with shrines located west of [[Tonegawa, Kōzuke|Tonegawa]] in [[:en:Kōzuke province|Kōzuke province]] (like Akagi Daimyōjin, Ikaho Daimyōjin and Komochiyama Daimyōjin), the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] and other [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] shrines, explaining the reason for their ''kami's'' rebirths, and telling tales about their previous lives.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The common point of the tales is that before being reborn as a tutelary ''kami'' of an area, a person must first be born and suffer there as a human being. The suffering is mostly caused by relationships with relatives, especially wives or husbands.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The book had a great impact on the literature and arts of the following centuries.
==References== {{Reflist}}
=== Works cited === {{Refbegin|colwidth=80em}} * {{cite book |last = Murakami |first = Manabu |author-link = Manabu Murakami (scholar) |chapter = ''Shintōshū'' |pages = 499 |title = [[Nihon Koten Bungaku Daijiten]] |script-title = ja:日本古典文学大辞典 |language = ja |year = 1983 |volume = 3 |location = Tokyo |publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten]] |oclc = 11917421 }} {{Refend}}

{{wikidata link|Q2616931}} [[Category:14th-century Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist texts]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Muromachi-period works]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto texts]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shintōshū]][[fr:Shintōshū]][[id:Shintōshū]][[ja:神道集]][[pt:Shintōshū]][[ru:Синтосю]][[uk:Сінтосю]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shintōshū]][[fr:Shintōshū]][[id:Shintōshū]][[ja:神道集]][[pt:Shintōshū]][[ru:Синтосю]][[uk:Сінтосю]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shintōshū]][[fr:Shintōshū]][[id:Shintōshū]][[ja:神道集]][[pt:Shintōshū]][[ru:Синтосю]][[uk:Сінтосю]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2616931]] Wikidata name: Shintōshū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shintōshū'''''|神道集}} is a Japanese ''[[setsuwa]]'' collection in ten volumes,{{sfnm|1a1=Murakami|1y=1983|1p=499}} believed to date from the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] (1336–1392).<ref name=kojien>Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> It illustrates with tales about various shrines the [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory, according to which Japanese ''[[kami]]'' were simply local manifestations of the Indian gods of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. This theory, created and developed mostly by [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] monks, was never systematized but was nonetheless very pervasive and influential.<ref>* {{cite web |url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm |title=Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami |last=Scheid, Bernhard |author-link=Bernhard Scheid |language=de |access-date=June 13, 2011}}</ref> The book had thereafter great influence over literature and the arts.<ref name="kojien"/>
==History== The book is believed to have been written during the late Nanboku-chō period, either during the [[:en:Bunna|Bunna]] or the [[:en:Enbun|Enbun]] era.<ref name=yahoo>{{cite web|last=Murakami|first=Manabu|title=Shintōshū|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E9%81%93%E9%9B%86/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121209055811/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E9%81%93%E9%9B%86/|url-status=dead|archive-date=9 December 2012|work=Shogakukan Encyclopedia|publisher=Yahoo Japan|access-date=15 June 2011|language=ja}}</ref> It carries the note {{nihongo|Agui-saku|安居院作|extra=made by Agui}} but who exactly wrote it is unclear. Divided into ten volumes and 50 chapters, it supports the Tendai and [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shinto]] ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory according to which Japanese ''[[kami]]'' were simply local manifestations of the Indian gods of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. This theory was never systematized but became nonetheless the most important tool through which foreign Buddhism was reconciled with local ''kami'' beliefs. The book illustrates it through tales dedicated to various shrines and to the Buddhist gods which are the true nature of the ''kami'' they enshrine.<ref name="yahoo"/> It deals mostly with shrines located west of [[Tonegawa, Kōzuke|Tonegawa]] in [[:en:Kōzuke province|Kōzuke province]] (like Akagi Daimyōjin, Ikaho Daimyōjin and Komochiyama Daimyōjin), the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] and other [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] shrines, explaining the reason for their ''kami's'' rebirths, and telling tales about their previous lives.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The common point of the tales is that before being reborn as a tutelary ''kami'' of an area, a person must first be born and suffer there as a human being. The suffering is mostly caused by relationships with relatives, especially wives or husbands.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The book had a great impact on the literature and arts of the following centuries.

=== Works cited === {{Refbegin|colwidth=80em}} * {{cite book |last = Murakami |first = Manabu |author-link = Manabu Murakami (scholar) |chapter = ''Shintōshū'' |pages = 499 |title = [[Nihon Koten Bungaku Daijiten]] |script-title = ja:日本古典文学大辞典 |language = ja |year = 1983 |volume = 3 |location = Tokyo |publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten]] |oclc = 11917421 }} {{Refend}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shintōshū]][[fr:Shintōshū]][[id:Shintōshū]][[ja:神道集]][[pt:Shintōshū]][[ru:Синтосю]][[uk:Сінтосю]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shintōshū]][[fr:Shintōshū]][[id:Shintōshū]][[ja:神道集]][[pt:Shintōshū]][[ru:Синтосю]][[uk:Сінтосю]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shintōshū]][[fr:Shintōshū]][[id:Shintōshū]][[ja:神道集]][[pt:Shintōshū]][[ru:Синтосю]][[uk:Сінтосю]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2616931]] Wikidata name: Shintōshū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shintōshū'''''|神道集}} is a Japanese ''[[setsuwa]]'' collection in ten volumes,{{sfnm|1a1=Murakami|1y=1983|1p=499}} believed to date from the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] (1336–1392).<ref name=kojien>Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> It illustrates with tales about various shrines the [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory, according to which Japanese ''[[kami]]'' were simply local manifestations of the Indian gods of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. This theory, created and developed mostly by [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] monks, was never systematized but was nonetheless very pervasive and influential.<ref>* {{cite web |url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm |title=Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami |last=Scheid, Bernhard |author-link=Bernhard Scheid |language=de |access-date=June 13, 2011}}</ref> The book had thereafter great influence over literature and the arts.<ref name="kojien"/>
==History== The book is believed to have been written during the late Nanboku-chō period, either during the [[:en:Bunna|Bunna]] or the [[:en:Enbun|Enbun]] era.<ref name=yahoo>{{cite web|last=Murakami|first=Manabu|title=Shintōshū|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E9%81%93%E9%9B%86/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121209055811/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E9%81%93%E9%9B%86/|url-status=dead|archive-date=9 December 2012|work=Shogakukan Encyclopedia|publisher=Yahoo Japan|access-date=15 June 2011|language=ja}}</ref> It carries the note {{nihongo|Agui-saku|安居院作|extra=made by Agui}} but who exactly wrote it is unclear. Divided into ten volumes and 50 chapters, it supports the Tendai and [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shinto]] ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory according to which Japanese ''[[kami]]'' were simply local manifestations of the Indian gods of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. This theory was never systematized but became nonetheless the most important tool through which foreign Buddhism was reconciled with local ''kami'' beliefs. The book illustrates it through tales dedicated to various shrines and to the Buddhist gods which are the true nature of the ''kami'' they enshrine.<ref name="yahoo"/> It deals mostly with shrines located west of [[Tonegawa, Kōzuke|Tonegawa]] in [[:en:Kōzuke province|Kōzuke province]] (like Akagi Daimyōjin, Ikaho Daimyōjin and Komochiyama Daimyōjin), the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] and other [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] shrines, explaining the reason for their ''kami's'' rebirths, and telling tales about their previous lives.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The common point of the tales is that before being reborn as a tutelary ''kami'' of an area, a person must first be born and suffer there as a human being. The suffering is mostly caused by relationships with relatives, especially wives or husbands.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The book had a great impact on the literature and arts of the following centuries.

This theory, created and developed mostly by {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}} monks, was never systematized but was nonetheless very pervasive and influential.<ref>* {{cite web |url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm |title=Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami |last=Scheid, Bernhard |author-link=Bernhard Scheid |language=de |access-date=June 13, 2011}}</ref> The book had thereafter great influence over literature and the arts.<ref name="kojien"/>
==History== The book is believed to have been written during the late Nanboku-chō period, either during the {{ill|Bunna|en|Bunna|WD=Q1146252|lt=Bunna}} or the {{ill|Enbun|en|Enbun|WD=Q1145071|lt=Enbun}} era.<ref name=yahoo>{{cite web|last=Murakami|first=Manabu|title=Shintōshū|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E9%81%93%E9%9B%86/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121209055811/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E9%81%93%E9%9B%86/|url-status=dead|archive-date=9 December 2012|work=Shogakukan Encyclopedia|publisher=Yahoo Japan|access-date=15 June 2011|language=ja}}</ref> It carries the note {{nihongo|Agui-saku|安居院作|extra=made by Agui}} but who exactly wrote it is unclear. Divided into ten volumes and 50 chapters, it supports the Tendai and [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shinto]] ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory according to which Japanese ''[[kami]]'' were simply local manifestations of the Indian gods of {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}}. This theory was never systematized but became nonetheless the most important tool through which foreign Buddhism was reconciled with local ''kami'' beliefs. The book illustrates it through tales dedicated to various shrines and to the Buddhist gods which are the true nature of the ''kami'' they enshrine.<ref name="yahoo"/> It deals mostly with shrines located west of [[Tonegawa, Kōzuke|Tonegawa]] in [[:en:Kōzuke province|Kōzuke province]] (like Akagi Daimyōjin, Ikaho Daimyōjin and Komochiyama Daimyōjin), the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] and other [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] shrines, explaining the reason for their ''kami's'' rebirths, and telling tales about their previous lives.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The common point of the tales is that before being reborn as a tutelary ''kami'' of an area, a person must first be born and suffer there as a human being. The suffering is mostly caused by relationships with relatives, especially wives or husbands.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The book had a great impact on the literature and arts of the following centuries.

Divided into ten volumes and 50 chapters, it supports the Tendai and [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shinto]] ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory according to which Japanese ''[[kami]]'' were simply local manifestations of the Indian gods of {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}. This theory was never systematized but became nonetheless the most important tool through which foreign Buddhism was reconciled with local ''kami'' beliefs. The book illustrates it through tales dedicated to various shrines and to the Buddhist gods which are the true nature of the ''kami'' they enshrine.<ref name="yahoo"/> It deals mostly with shrines located west of [[Tonegawa, Kōzuke|Tonegawa]] in [[:en:Kōzuke province|Kōzuke province]] (like Akagi Daimyōjin, Ikaho Daimyōjin and Komochiyama Daimyōjin), the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] and other [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] shrines, explaining the reason for their ''kami's'' rebirths, and telling tales about their previous lives.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The common point of the tales is that before being reborn as a tutelary ''kami'' of an area, a person must first be born and suffer there as a human being. The suffering is mostly caused by relationships with relatives, especially wives or husbands.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The book had a great impact on the literature and arts of the following centuries.
==References== {{Reflist}}
=== Works cited === {{Refbegin|colwidth=80em}} * {{cite book |last = Murakami |first = Manabu |author-link = Manabu Murakami (scholar) |chapter = ''Shintōshū'' |pages = 499 |title = [[Nihon Koten Bungaku Daijiten]] |script-title = ja:日本古典文学大辞典 |language = ja |year = 1983 |volume = 3 |location = Tokyo |publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten]] |oclc = 11917421 }} {{Refend}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shintōshū]][[fr:Shintōshū]][[id:Shintōshū]][[ja:神道集]][[pt:Shintōshū]][[ru:Синтосю]][[uk:Сінтосю]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shintōshū]][[fr:Shintōshū]][[id:Shintōshū]][[ja:神道集]][[pt:Shintōshū]][[ru:Синтосю]][[uk:Сінтосю]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shintōshū]][[fr:Shintōshū]][[id:Shintōshū]][[ja:神道集]][[pt:Shintōshū]][[ru:Синтосю]][[uk:Сінтосю]]
{{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shintōshū'''''|神道集}} is a Japanese ''[[setsuwa]]'' collection in ten volumes,{{sfnm|1a1=Murakami|1y=1983|1p=499}} believed to date from the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}} (1336–1392).<ref name=kojien>Iwanami {{nihongo|{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|WD=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien|1=Kōjien}}|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> It illustrates with tales about various shrines the {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism in Japan}} ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory, according to which Japanese ''[[kami]]'' were simply local manifestations of the Indian gods of {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}. This theory, created and developed mostly by {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} monks, was never systematized but was nonetheless very pervasive and influential.<ref>* {{cite web |url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm |title=Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami |last=Scheid, Bernhard |author-link=Bernhard Scheid |language=de |access-date=June 13, 2011}}</ref> The book had thereafter great influence over literature and the arts.<ref name="kojien"/>
==History== The book is believed to have been written during the late Nanboku-chō period, either during the {{ill|Bunna|en|Bunna|WD=Q1146252|lt=Bunna|1=Bunna}} or the {{ill|Enbun|en|Enbun|WD=Q1145071|lt=Enbun|1=Enbun}} era.<ref name=yahoo>{{cite web|last=Murakami|first=Manabu|title=Shintōshū|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E9%81%93%E9%9B%86/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121209055811/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E9%81%93%E9%9B%86/|url-status=dead|archive-date=9 December 2012|work=Shogakukan Encyclopedia|publisher=Yahoo Japan|access-date=15 June 2011|language=ja}}</ref> It carries the note {{nihongo|Agui-saku|安居院作|extra=made by Agui}} but who exactly wrote it is unclear. Divided into ten volumes and 50 chapters, it supports the Tendai and [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shinto]] ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory according to which Japanese ''[[kami]]'' were simply local manifestations of the Indian gods of {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}. This theory was never systematized but became nonetheless the most important tool through which foreign Buddhism was reconciled with local ''kami'' beliefs. The book illustrates it through tales dedicated to various shrines and to the Buddhist gods which are the true nature of the ''kami'' they enshrine.<ref name="yahoo"/> It deals mostly with shrines located west of [[Tonegawa, Kōzuke|Tonegawa]] in [[:en:Kōzuke province|Kōzuke province]] (like Akagi Daimyōjin, Ikaho Daimyōjin and Komochiyama Daimyōjin), the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] and other [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] shrines, explaining the reason for their ''kami's'' rebirths, and telling tales about their previous lives.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The common point of the tales is that before being reborn as a tutelary ''kami'' of an area, a person must first be born and suffer there as a human being. The suffering is mostly caused by relationships with relatives, especially wives or husbands.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The book had a great impact on the literature and arts of the following centuries.
